section: Strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels

context_update.summary: The introduction and experimental procedures of a study on strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al ferritic low-density steels are presented. Binary Fe-Al alloys with 5, 6, 6.8, and 9 wt% Al were arc-melted and remelted to reduce segregation. The Fe-6.8Al alloy was cold-rolled 98% and annealed at 750 °C for 5 and 60 min. Powders for Mössbauer and SAXS were filed and annealed at 600 °C for 60 min under vacuum. Characterization includes Mössbauer (57Co source, α-Fe calibration), SAXS (dual Mo-Cr energy, camera lengths 550 and 2400 mm, q 0.0254–10 nm⁻¹, FIT2D integration, glassy carbon and Silver-Behenate standards), XRD (Cu-Kα, 40–120° 2θ, step 0.03°, (110) peak for dislocation density via Variance method), SEM, TEM (specific electropolishing), and EPMA. Tensile tests used sub-size specimens (gauge 6 mm × 2 mm) along RD at 10⁻³ s⁻¹. The work attributes high yield strength to ferrite grain size, dislocations, ordered Fe3Al phase, and solid solution strengthening, modelled with Hall-Petch and Taylor equations. No microstructural images, phase quantifications, or mechanical property values appear in this chunk; results are expected next. Key aliases: Fe-5Al, Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al, Fe-9Al, 98%CR, annealed 750°C 5min/60min.
processing_route: vacuum arc melting with multiple remelting
powder preparation: filing, sealing under vacuum, annealing 600 °C, 60 min
mechanisms: enhanced yield strength from ferrite grain size, dislocations, ordered Fe3Al, solid solution strengthening

section: 2. Experimental procedure and methods of analyses

context_update.summary: This chunk provides experimental details and initial results. The Fe-6.8Al alloy is processed by 98% cold rolling and annealing at 750°C for 5 and 60 min. XRD, SEM, tensile testing and TEM conditions are specified. Mössbauer spectra are analyzed using NORMOS with up to 40 sextets and a hyperfine field model B = B0 - n1ΔB1 - n2ΔB2 (B0=33 T, ΔB1=2.4 T, ΔB2=0.5 T). SAXS line profile analysis employs a local monodisperse model for DO3 ordered phase. XRD line profile analysis uses restricted moments (second and fourth order) to separate crystallite size and dislocation density. The results present Fig. 1 with Mössbauer spectra and Bhf distributions for α-Fe and Fe-5Al, Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al, Fe-9Al. Table 1 lists hyperfine fields, spectral areas, volume fractions and atomic configuration assignments. Key observations: a peak at 30.6 T in all Fe-Al alloys possibly indicating Fe3Al or solid solution configuration; low-intensity peaks at ~23 T in Al≥6% samples suggesting Fe3Al with low volume fraction, absent in Fe-5Al. The decrease in Bhf with Al content is noted. Subsequent chunks need to connect these microstructural fingerprints to dislocation density and mechanical properties.
processing_route: annealing at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min
key_variables: Mössbauer hyperfine parameters B0, ΔB1, ΔB2
microstructure: ordered Fe3Al phase presence suggested by Mössbauer peaks
mechanisms: hyperfine field decrease with Al content
Fe3Al phase indication through specific atomic configurations

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Mössbauer spectroscopy (Section 3.1.1), SAXS (3.1.2), and electron microscopy (3.1.3) results for Fe-Al low-density steels. Mössbauer analysis reveals: pure Fe shows Bhf peak at 33 T (bcc configuration); Fe-Al alloys exhibit multiple Bhf peaks due to Al-induced local configuration changes, with Bhf decreasing as Al neighbours increase. A peak at 30.6 T in all Fe-Al alloys could arise from either (7Fe+1Al)nn+(6Fe)nnn configuration or Fe3Al (8Fe)nn+(6Al)nnn. Low-intensity peaks at ~23 T in Al ≥6 wt% samples suggest low-volume-fraction Fe3Al, absent in Fe-5Al. SAXS shows radially symmetric profiles; a weak peak at q=0.3–3 nm⁻¹ from nano-sized ordered phase intensifies with Al content, indicating growth. Ordered phase diameter estimated via SAXS fitting increases from 2.5±0.15 nm (Fe-5Al) to 3.75±0.2 nm (Fe-9Al). SAXS volume fractions are lower than Mössbauer’s due to difference in probing sharp vs. diffused boundaries. SEM of Fe-6.8Al: 98% cold rolled condition exhibits elongated grains, shear bands at ~40° to RD, lamellae width 2.6±1.2 µm. Annealing at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min yields equiaxed recrystallised grains with sizes 25.2±5.0 µm and 48.3±1.2 µm, respectively (Table 2). XRD crystallite size for 98%CR is 102.3±9.8 nm. TEM dark-field imaging confirms very fine ordered phase size of 2.1±0.2 nm, consistent with SAXS. Key figures: Fig. 1–6, Table 2. For subsequent sections, the connection between these microstructural findings and mechanical properties, particularly dislocation density and strengthening models, should be established.
processing_route: vacuum arc melting and multiple remelting
annealing at 750 °C for 5 min and 60 min (Fe-6.8Al bulk)
powder filing, vacuum sealing, annealing at 600 °C for 60 min (for Mössbauer)
microstructure: shear bands at ~40° to RD in 98%CR Fe-6.8Al
mechanisms: hyperfine field decrease with increasing Al neighbour count
ordered phase nucleation driven by Al content
SAXS detects only sharp-boundary ordered phase; Mössbauer also detects diffuse-boundary configurations

context_update.summary: Current chunk provides TEM confirmation that the ordered phase size in Fe-6.8Al is 2.1±0.2 nm, consistent with SAXS. XRD analysis of Fe-6.8Al (98%CR, 750°C 5 min, 750°C 60 min) measures crystallite size (102.3±9.8 nm for 98%CR) and micro-strain, revealing a decrease from 1.46×10⁻³ (98%CR) to 9.46×10⁻⁴ (5 min) to 1.46×10⁻⁴ (60 min) as recrystallization proceeds. Dislocation density derived from XRD line profiles drops sharply from ~2.2×10¹⁵ m⁻² (98%CR) to ~10¹² m⁻² (60 min). Mechanical properties show YS = 1800 MPa for 98%CR, 700 MPa (5 min), 265 MPa (60 min); UTS = 870 MPa (5 min), 394 MPa (60 min); uniform elongation = 1.2%, 20.0%, 29.8%. Yield ratios are 0.85 (5 min) and 0.67 (60 min). Strain hardening rate is higher for the 60 min annealed state. Dislocation strengthening is identified as the main mechanism for the high strength in the cold rolled condition. The relationships among processing, dislocation density, and yield strength are directly demonstrated. Charts and tables: Fig. 7, Table 3 (micro-strain, crystallite size), Fig. 8, Table 4 (mechanical properties). For subsequent sections, these quantitative links between dislocation density/strain and strength should be incorporated into strengthening models.
microstructure: recrystallization and grain growth upon annealing
properties: YS, UTS, uniform elongation, yield ratio (Table 4)
strain hardening rate: 60 min > 5 min, sudden increase then gradual decrease
mechanisms: dislocation strengthening identified as main mechanism
recrystallization reduces micro-strain and dislocation density
ordered phase size confirmed by TEM and SAXS

Microstructure: ordered Fe3Al phase presence suggested by Mössbauer peaks; Bhf distributions for all alloys; ferrite matrix (disordered Fe-Al); nano-sized ordered Fe3Al phase (DO3); shear bands at ~40° to RD in 98%CR Fe-6.8Al; deformed lamellae width 2.6±1.2 µm; equiaxed recrystallised grains (25.2–48.3 µm); crystallite size 102.3±9.8 nm (XRD) for 98%CR
Properties: YS, UTS, uniform elongation, yield ratio (Table 4); strain hardening rate: 60 min > 5 min, sudden increase then gradual decrease

context_update.summary: This chunk (Discussion and Conclusions) deconstructs the yield strength of Fe-6.8Al into contributions from five strengthening mechanisms: dislocations, grain boundaries, ordered Fe3Al phase, solid solution, and lattice friction. Explicit formulas and parameter values are provided: solid solution strengthening τ_ss = 95.6 MPa (G=82 GPa, c=6.8 wt%, ε_s=0.45), span of 67.8–127.2 MPa for 5–9 wt% Al; lattice friction τ_PN ~154.34 MPa at 0 K but 41 MPa used at room temperature; ordered phase strength via τ_order = 0.7G(γ/Gb)^{3/2}(f r/b)^{1/2}; Hall-Petch grain boundary strengthening with constant from 99.95% Fe; dislocation strengthening via Taylor equation with α=0.33. Figure 9 quantifies these contributions for 98%CR, 750°C 5 min, and 60 min conditions, showing dislocation plus grain boundary dominant in CR, shifting to ordered phase plus solid solution after 60 min annealing. Ordered Fe3Al domains have sizes 2.5–3.75 nm and the strengthening mechanism involves antiphase boundaries and super-partial dislocations. A comparison with Rana et al. (YS 342 MPa at 900°C 1 min) highlights Hall-Petch effect via finer grain size (25.5 vs 69.2 µm). The single-phase alloy achieves YS 700 MPa and 20% elongation, rivalling dual-phase grades. The conclusions stress that major strength contributions come from dislocations and grain size, with nano-ordered phase as an additional factor, validated by TEM, SAXS, Mössbauer and numerical strengthening models.
processing_route: annealing at 750 °C for 5 and 60 min (Fe-6.8Al bulk)
key_variables: ordered phase fraction f and radius r
microstructure: ferrite grain size in annealed Fe-6.8Al: 25.5 ± 5 µm (750°C 5 min)
ferrite grain size in literature: 69.2 ± 34.8 µm (900°C 1 min)
properties: solid solution strengthening τ_ss = 95.6 MPa (Fe-6.8Al), range 67.8–127.2 MPa (5–9 wt% Al)
lattice frictional stress 41 MPa at room temperature
mechanisms: dislocation strengthening dominant in cold rolled state
ordered phase and solid solution strengthening dominant after 60 min annealing
ordered phase strengthening via super-partial dislocations and antiphase boundaries
solid solution strengthening modeled by τ_ss = (G ε_s^{3/2} c^{1/2})/700

(YS 342 MPa at 900°C 1 min) highlights Hall-Petch effect via finer grain size (25.5 vs 69.2 µm). The single-phase alloy achieves YS 700 MPa and 20% elongation, rivalling dual-phase grades.
